main_paper.raw: {"methods":["Context-aware dynamic pruning technique for speech foundation models","Module-level mask prediction for encoder and decoder layers based on context (speech features, language, task)","Simultaneous fine-tuning of the foundation model with pruning"],"problems":["High computational cost and inference time of large speech foundation models in real-world applications","Existing pruning and efficiency techniques are mostly applied during training and not adapted to context during inference","Lack of optimal model structure adaptation for different tasks, languages, and speaker characteristics"],"datasets":[],"metrics":["Inference time reduction (%)","BLEU score (for Speech Translation, ST)","Word Error Rate (WER, for Automatic Speech Recognition, ASR)"],"results":[{"metric":"Inference time reduction (ST task)","value":"34.3%"},{"metric":"BLEU score degradation (ST task)","value":"No degradation"},{"metric":"Inference time reduction (ASR task)","value":"28.6%"},{"metric":"WER degradation (ASR task)","value":"2.8%"}],"novelty_claims":["First to propose context-aware dynamic pruning at inference time for speech foundation models, adapting to speaker, language, and task context.","Pruning is performed dynamically during inference, not just during training, without altering the underlying model.","Demonstrates that pruned structures can be interpreted and that task-related information is a dominant factor for efficient pruning in multilingual/multi-task scenarios."]}

methods: Context-aware dynamic pruning technique for speech foundation models
Module-level mask prediction for encoder and decoder layers based on context (speech features, language, task)
Simultaneous fine-tuning of the foundation model with pruning
problems: High computational cost and inference time of large speech foundation models in real-world applications
Existing pruning and efficiency techniques are mostly applied during training and not adapted to context during inference
Lack of optimal model structure adaptation for different tasks, languages, and speaker characteristics
metrics: Word Error Rate (WER, for Automatic Speech Recognition, ASR)
novelty_claims: First to propose context-aware dynamic pruning at inference time for speech foundation models, adapting to speaker, language, and task context.
Pruning is performed dynamically during inference, not just during training, without altering the underlying model.
Demonstrates that pruned structures can be interpreted and that task-related information is a dominant factor for efficient pruning in multilingual/multi-task scenarios.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Systematic analysis and survey of SFM+LLM (Speech Foundation Model + Large Language Model) architectures for speech-to-text translation","Identification and abstraction of common architectural building blocks in SFM+LLM models","Comparative analysis of training strategies and evaluation approaches used in SFM+LLM-based ST models","Recommendations for standardizing training and evaluation settings for future research"],"problems":["Lack of unified view and comparability among SFM+LLM-based speech-to-text translation models due to diverse architectures and evaluation strategies","Difficulty in identifying the best-performing architectural and training choices for SFM+LLM models in speech translation","Absence of standard training and evaluation settings for fair comparison of SFM+LLM solutions"],"datasets":[],"metrics":[],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["First systematic analysis and unified abstraction of SFM+LLM architectures for speech-to-text translation","Identification of five common architectural building blocks across surveyed models","Highlighting methodological inconsistencies and the need for standardization in training and evaluation for SFM+LLM-based ST","Providing recommendations and best practices for future research to enable fair and insightful evaluation of SFM+LLM models"]}

methods: Systematic analysis and survey of SFM+LLM (Speech Foundation Model + Large Language Model) architectures for speech-to-text translation
Identification and abstraction of common architectural building blocks in SFM+LLM models
Comparative analysis of training strategies and evaluation approaches used in SFM+LLM-based ST models
Recommendations for standardizing training and evaluation settings for future research
problems: Lack of unified view and comparability among SFM+LLM-based speech-to-text translation models due to diverse architectures and evaluation strategies
Difficulty in identifying the best-performing architectural and training choices for SFM+LLM models in speech translation
Absence of standard training and evaluation settings for fair comparison of SFM+LLM solutions
novelty_claims: First systematic analysis and unified abstraction of SFM+LLM architectures for speech-to-text translation
Identification of five common architectural building blocks across surveyed models
Highlighting methodological inconsistencies and the need for standardization in training and evaluation for SFM+LLM-based ST
Providing recommendations and best practices for future research to enable fair and insightful evaluation of SFM+LLM models

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Speech processing Universal PERformance Benchmark (SUPERB) ecosystem for evaluation","Unified learning framework with frozen foundation model and task-specialized lightweight prediction heads","Learnable weighted-sum over all frozen layers for each task","Layer-wise single-layer benchmarking for specific tasks (e.g., voice conversion)","Community-driven online leaderboard and benchmark database"],"problems":["Lack of a standardized, large-scale, multi-task evaluation setup for speech foundation models","Difficulty in assessing generalizability of speech self-supervised learning (SSL) models across diverse speech processing tasks","Inconsistent evaluation protocols and datasets in prior speech SSL research","Need for efficient transfer of knowledge from foundation models to downstream speech tasks with minimal resources"],"datasets":["LibriSpeech (for pre-training and evaluation)","SUPERB benchmark tasks (15 tasks, each with its own dataset, e.g., PR, KS, SID, ER, IC, SF, ASR, SV, SD, QbE, ST, OOD-ASR, SS, SE, VC)"],"metrics":["Task-specific metrics for each of the 15 SUPERB tasks (e.g., accuracy, F1 score, word error rate, equal error rate, etc.)","Statistical significance tests for model comparisons"],"results":[{"metric":"Generalizability across 15 SUPERB tasks","value":"Top SSL models achieve near or better than non-SSL approaches on most tasks"},{"metric":"Effect of model scaling","value":"Scaling up the foundation model consistently improves performance across all tasks"},{"metric":"Layer selection protocol","value":"Learnable weighted-sum over all layers outperforms using only the last layer; single-layer benchmarking can sometimes yield even better results"},{"metric":"Community engagement","value":"33 models evaluated, including 14 new community submissions since leaderboard launch"}],"novelty_claims":["First large-scale, standardized benchmark (SUPERB) for evaluating speech foundation models across 15 diverse tasks","Unified framework for evaluating frozen speech foundation models with lightweight, task-specific heads and learnable layer weighting","Demonstration that SSL models can generalize well across a wide range of speech tasks, not just ASR","Empirical finding that layer-wise benchmarking and learnable weighted-sum protocols outperform conventional last-layer evaluation","Provision of a community-driven platform with an online leaderboard and open evaluation codebase to foster collaboration and reproducibility","Analysis of statistical significance and robustness in model comparisons, with recommendations for best practices"]}

methods: Speech processing Universal PERformance Benchmark (SUPERB) ecosystem for evaluation
Unified learning framework with frozen foundation model and task-specialized lightweight prediction heads
Learnable weighted-sum over all frozen layers for each task
Layer-wise single-layer benchmarking for specific tasks (e.g., voice conversion)
Community-driven online leaderboard and benchmark database
problems: Lack of a standardized, large-scale, multi-task evaluation setup for speech foundation models
Difficulty in assessing generalizability of speech self-supervised learning (SSL) models across diverse speech processing tasks
Inconsistent evaluation protocols and datasets in prior speech SSL research
Need for efficient transfer of knowledge from foundation models to downstream speech tasks with minimal resources
datasets: SUPERB benchmark tasks (15 tasks, each with its own dataset, e.g., PR, KS, SID, ER, IC, SF, ASR, SV, SD, QbE, ST, OOD-ASR, SS, SE, VC)
metrics: Task-specific metrics for each of the 15 SUPERB tasks (e.g., accuracy, F1 score, word error rate, equal error rate, etc.)
Statistical significance tests for model comparisons

results.value: Top SSL models achieve near or better than non-SSL approaches on most tasks
Scaling up the foundation model consistently improves performance across all tasks
Learnable weighted-sum over all layers outperforms using only the last layer; single-layer benchmarking can sometimes yield even better results
33 models evaluated, including 14 new community submissions since leaderboard launch

novelty_claims: First large-scale, standardized benchmark (SUPERB) for evaluating speech foundation models across 15 diverse tasks
Unified framework for evaluating frozen speech foundation models with lightweight, task-specific heads and learnable layer weighting
Demonstration that SSL models can generalize well across a wide range of speech tasks, not just ASR
Empirical finding that layer-wise benchmarking and learnable weighted-sum protocols outperform conventional last-layer evaluation
Provision of a community-driven platform with an online leaderboard and open evaluation codebase to foster collaboration and reproducibility
Analysis of statistical significance and robustness in model comparisons, with recommendations for best practices

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["WavLLM: a robust and adaptive speech large language model with dual encoders (Whisper for semantic content, WavLM for speaker/acoustic features)","Prompt-aware LoRA weight adapter for adaptive tuning based on prompts","Two-stage curriculum learning approach: initial mixed single-task training followed by advanced multi-task training","Decoupling of speech information via dual encoders","Chain-of-Thought (CoT) approach for complex speech tasks"],"problems":["Generalization of speech LLMs across varied contexts and complex auditory tasks","Sensitivity of specialized speech tasks to prompt design, leading to performance degradation on unseen/complex instructions","Lack of speech Chain-of-Thought (CoT) capability in previous models","Robustly following speech instructions and supporting multi-round dialog in LLMs"],"datasets":["Universal speech benchmarks (including ASR, ST, SV, ER tasks)","Gaokao English listening comprehension set (for SQA)","Speech Chain-of-Thought (CoT) evaluation set","Synthesized spoken question-answering dataset generated by GPT-4","Prompt-aware multi-task speech processing dataset"],"metrics":["State-of-the-art (SOTA) performance on speech tasks (relative to model size)","Generalization capability (robustness to unseen/complex prompts)","Zero-shot performance on SQA (Gaokao tasks)","Performance on CoT-based tasks vs. non-CoT tasks"],"results":[{"metric":"State-of-the-art performance (relative to model size)","value":"Achieved across a range of speech tasks"},{"metric":"Zero-shot SQA (Gaokao tasks)","value":"Successfully completed without specialized training"},{"metric":"Generalization on CoT tasks","value":"Performance gains over non-CoT tasks; robust generalization demonstrated"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of a dual-encoder architecture (Whisper for semantics, WavLM for speaker/acoustic features) to decouple speech information for LLMs","Development of a prompt-aware LoRA weight adapter for adaptive prompt-based tuning during multi-task training","Application of a two-stage curriculum learning approach (from simple to complex/multi-task) for robust and generalizable speech LLMs","Demonstration of strong Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning capabilities in speech tasks","First to achieve strong zero-shot performance on Gaokao English listening comprehension tasks without specialized training"]}

methods: WavLLM: a robust and adaptive speech large language model with dual encoders (Whisper for semantic content, WavLM for speaker/acoustic features)

problems: Generalization of speech LLMs across varied contexts and complex auditory tasks

datasets: Universal speech benchmarks (including ASR, ST, SV, ER tasks)
Gaokao English listening comprehension set (for SQA)
Synthesized spoken question-answering dataset generated by GPT-4
metrics: State-of-the-art (SOTA) performance on speech tasks (relative to model size)

results.value: Achieved across a range of speech tasks
Performance gains over non-CoT tasks; robust generalization demonstrated

novelty_claims: Introduction of a dual-encoder architecture (Whisper for semantics, WavLM for speaker/acoustic features) to decouple speech information for LLMs
Development of a prompt-aware LoRA weight adapter for adaptive prompt-based tuning during multi-task training
Application of a two-stage curriculum learning approach (from simple to complex/multi-task) for robust and generalizable speech LLMs
Demonstration of strong Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning capabilities in speech tasks
First to achieve strong zero-shot performance on Gaokao English listening comprehension tasks without specialized training

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Transfer learning using speech foundation models (wav2vec 2.0)","Systematic evaluation of layer selection for feature extraction from foundation models","Training classifiers to predict fine-grained pathological speech features from different model layers","Learned weighted sum of representations from all layers as an alternative to single-layer selection"],"problems":["Automatic and objective assessment of clinical speech for diagnosis and treatment of speech disorders","Improving generalizability and performance of speech models for clinical applications, especially with limited clinical data","Determining the optimal layer(s) of speech foundation models for predicting pathological speech features"],"datasets":["A novel clinical dataset of pathological speech recordings (details not specified in the excerpt)"],"metrics":["Balanced accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"Balanced accuracy (best layer vs. worst layer)","value":"~15.8% increase per feature"},{"metric":"Balanced accuracy (best layer vs. final layer)","value":"~13.6% increase per feature"},{"metric":"Balanced accuracy (learned weighted sum vs. average best layer, in-distribution)","value":"~1.2% lower"},{"metric":"Balanced accuracy (learned weighted sum vs. average best layer, out-of-distribution)","value":"1.5% lower"}],"novelty_claims":["First systematic exploration of the effect of layer selection in speech foundation models (wav2vec 2.0) for predicting fine-grained pathological speech features in a clinical context.","Demonstration that optimal layer selection can significantly improve performance for pathological speech feature prediction, with improvements up to ~15.8% in balanced accuracy.","Evaluation of a learned weighted sum of all layers as a robust alternative to single-layer selection, showing strong generalization to out-of-distribution data."]}

results.metric: Balanced accuracy (best layer vs. worst layer)
Balanced accuracy (best layer vs. final layer)

novelty_claims: First systematic exploration of the effect of layer selection in speech foundation models (wav2vec 2.0) for predicting fine-grained pathological speech features in a clinical context.
Demonstration that optimal layer selection can significantly improve performance for pathological speech feature prediction, with improvements up to ~15.8% in balanced accuracy.
Evaluation of a learned weighted sum of all layers as a robust alternative to single-layer selection, showing strong generalization to out-of-distribution data.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["HJ-Pruning (Heterogeneous Joint Pruning) for both CNN and Transformer components in SSL speech models","HJ-Pruning based on overall model size (single sparsity for entire model)","HJ-Pruning based on separate model sizes (separate sparsity for CNN and Transformer)","HJ-Pruning based on overall MACs (sparsity based on multiply-accumulate operations)"],"problems":["High computational cost and large memory requirements of self-supervised pre-trained speech models (e.g., wav2vec2, Hubert, WavLM)","Inefficiency of existing pruning methods that only target Transformer layers and ignore the computationally expensive CNN frontend","Lack of structured pruning methods that jointly optimize both CNN and Transformer components for speech models"],"datasets":["LibriSpeech","SLURP"],"metrics":["Word Error Rate (WER)","Multiply-Accumulate operations (MACs)","Model size"],"results":[{"metric":"Computation reduction (MACs) with no accuracy degradation","value":"40% to 50% less computation than baseline"},{"metric":"Computation reduction (MACs) with improved accuracy","value":"10% to 30% less computation and more accurate than original wav2vec2-base"}],"novelty_claims":["First structured pruning method that jointly prunes both CNN and Transformer components in self-supervised speech models","Proposes three task-specific structured pruning strategies for heterogeneous networks in speech models","Demonstrates that pruning the CNN frontend, despite its small parameter count, yields significant computational savings","Shows that HJ-Pruning outperforms previous Transformer-only pruning strategies in both accuracy and computational efficiency"]}

methods: HJ-Pruning (Heterogeneous Joint Pruning) for both CNN and Transformer components in SSL speech models
HJ-Pruning based on overall model size (single sparsity for entire model)
HJ-Pruning based on separate model sizes (separate sparsity for CNN and Transformer)
HJ-Pruning based on overall MACs (sparsity based on multiply-accumulate operations)
problems: High computational cost and large memory requirements of self-supervised pre-trained speech models (e.g., wav2vec2, Hubert, WavLM)
Inefficiency of existing pruning methods that only target Transformer layers and ignore the computationally expensive CNN frontend
Lack of structured pruning methods that jointly optimize both CNN and Transformer components for speech models

results.metric: Computation reduction (MACs) with no accuracy degradation

results.value: 10% to 30% less computation and more accurate than original wav2vec2-base

novelty_claims: First structured pruning method that jointly prunes both CNN and Transformer components in self-supervised speech models
Proposes three task-specific structured pruning strategies for heterogeneous networks in speech models
Demonstrates that pruning the CNN frontend, despite its small parameter count, yields significant computational savings
Shows that HJ-Pruning outperforms previous Transformer-only pruning strategies in both accuracy and computational efficiency

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Fine-grained attention head pruning method for structured pruning","Introduction of the straight through estimator (STE) into L0 regularization for pruning","Task-agnostic structured pruning of pre-trained speech models"],"problems":["Large memory and computational requirements of self-supervised pre-trained speech models hinder industrial applicability","Structured pruning typically results in significant accuracy loss compared to unstructured pruning","Existing L0-regularization-based pruning methods are task-specific and computationally expensive"],"datasets":["SUPERB benchmark"],"metrics":["Model accuracy on downstream tasks (as measured by SUPERB)","Number of model parameters","Inference speed"],"results":[{"metric":"Parameter reduction","value":"72% fewer parameters compared to dense model"},{"metric":"Inference speed","value":"2 times faster than dense model"},{"metric":"Performance","value":"Comparable to dense model on multiple tasks; outperforms Wav2vec 2.0 base model on average"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a fine-grained attention head pruning method to reduce performance degradation in structured pruning of speech models","Introduces the straight through estimator (STE) into L0 regularization to further accelerate pruned models","Achieves task-agnostic structured pruning, enabling the pruned model to generalize across multiple downstream tasks without task-specific adaptation"]}

problems: Large memory and computational requirements of self-supervised pre-trained speech models hinder industrial applicability

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["S3-Router framework: finetuning model structure via learning a binary mask on top of pretrained model weights instead of finetuning weights directly","Novel mask initialization strategy for the pretrain-finetune paradigm","Learning language-/task-specific binary masks for multilingual/multitask speech processing","ASR pruning technique using binary masks to reduce model complexity while maintaining accuracy"],"problems":["Large size and inefficiency of advanced speech self-supervised learning (SSL) models for on-device and edge deployment","Overfitting of overparameterized speech SSL models when finetuned on low-resource speech corpora","Inefficiency and impracticality of deploying separate models for each language/task in multilingual/multitask scenarios","Lack of tools to quantitatively analyze what is encoded in speech SSL models"],"datasets":[],"metrics":[],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["Proposes S3-Router, a framework that finetunes the structure of speech SSL models via binary masks, rather than the weights, for downstream tasks.","First to show that discarding no more than 10% of model weights via binary masks (without finetuning weights) can outperform standard weight finetuning on downstream speech tasks, especially in low-resource settings.","Introduces a novel mask initialization strategy tailored for the pretrain-finetune paradigm in speech SSL.","Provides an all-in-one solution for efficient multilingual/multitask speech processing by learning language-/task-specific binary masks on shared model weights.","Demonstrates S3-Router as a state-of-the-art ASR pruning technique, achieving better performance-efficiency trade-offs than existing methods.","Presents S3-Router as a new tool for quantitatively analyzing the information encoded in speech SSL models due to the interpretability of binary masks."]}

methods: S3-Router framework: finetuning model structure via learning a binary mask on top of pretrained model weights instead of finetuning weights directly
Novel mask initialization strategy for the pretrain-finetune paradigm
Learning language-/task-specific binary masks for multilingual/multitask speech processing
ASR pruning technique using binary masks to reduce model complexity while maintaining accuracy
problems: Large size and inefficiency of advanced speech self-supervised learning (SSL) models for on-device and edge deployment
Overfitting of overparameterized speech SSL models when finetuned on low-resource speech corpora
Inefficiency and impracticality of deploying separate models for each language/task in multilingual/multitask scenarios
Lack of tools to quantitatively analyze what is encoded in speech SSL models
novelty_claims: Proposes S3-Router, a framework that finetunes the structure of speech SSL models via binary masks, rather than the weights, for downstream tasks.

First to show that discarding no more than 10% of model weights via binary masks (without finetuning weights) can outperform standard weight finetuning on downstream speech tasks, especially in low-resource settings.
Introduces a novel mask initialization strategy tailored for the pretrain-finetune paradigm in speech SSL.
Provides an all-in-one solution for efficient multilingual/multitask speech processing by learning language-/task-specific binary masks on shared model weights.
Demonstrates S3-Router as a state-of-the-art ASR pruning technique, achieving better performance-efficiency trade-offs than existing methods.
Presents S3-Router as a new tool for quantitatively analyzing the information encoded in speech SSL models due to the interpretability of binary masks.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Intermediate CTC regularization during training","Stochastic depth regularization during training","On-demand layer pruning (removing layers at inference without fine-tuning)","Singular Vector Canonical Correlation Analysis (SVCCA) for layer analysis","Iterative search method for safe layer pruning"],"problems":["Deploying end-to-end ASR models on devices with varying computational power and energy constraints","Reducing model depth at run-time without extra fine-tuning (on-demand pruning)","Maintaining ASR accuracy after pruning layers from the model"],"datasets":[],"metrics":["Real-time factor (RTF)","Recognition accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"Real-time factor (RTF) on GPU","value":"improved from 0.005 to 0.002"},{"metric":"Recognition accuracy of pruned sub-models","value":"matches accuracy of individually trained models of same depth"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a method to train ASR models (using intermediate CTC and stochastic depth) that can be pruned at run-time to various depths without any fine-tuning.","Demonstrates that pruned sub-models maintain the accuracy of individually trained models of the same depth.","Employs SVCCA to analyze the effect of intermediate CTC and stochastic depth in the context of layer pruning.","Presents an iterative search method augmented with intermediate CTC for identifying layers safe to prune."]}

methods: Intermediate CTC regularization during training
Stochastic depth regularization during training
On-demand layer pruning (removing layers at inference without fine-tuning)

Iterative search method for safe layer pruning
problems: Deploying end-to-end ASR models on devices with varying computational power and energy constraints

Maintaining ASR accuracy after pruning layers from the model

results.value: matches accuracy of individually trained models of same depth

novelty_claims: Proposes a method to train ASR models (using intermediate CTC and stochastic depth) that can be pruned at run-time to various depths without any fine-tuning.
Demonstrates that pruned sub-models maintain the accuracy of individually trained models of the same depth.
Employs SVCCA to analyze the effect of intermediate CTC and stochastic depth in the context of layer pruning.
Presents an iterative search method augmented with intermediate CTC for identifying layers safe to prune.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Prune-Adjust-Re-Prune (PARP): a magnitude-based unstructured pruning method for discovering and finetuning sparse subnetworks in pre-trained speech SSL (ASR) using self-supervised speech representation learning (SSL)","Finding sparse subnetworks in pre-trained speech SSL models that outperform the original dense models for low-resource ASR","Reducing error"],"results":[{"metric":"Word Error Rate (WER) decrease (Librispeech 10min, no LM decoding)","value":"10.9%/12.6% absolute decrease compared to the full model using PARP"}],"novelty_claims":["PARP (Prune-Adjust-Re-Prune) is a new pruning method that discovers and finetunes sparse subnetworks in pre-trained speech SSL models with only a single downstream ASR finetuning run, unlike prior methods requiring multiple runs.","PARP allows pruned-out weights to be revived during finetuning, enabling minimal adjustment and better performance than OMP/IMP.","PARP achieves both computational efficiency and superior ASR performance (lower WER) compared to baseline pruning methods.","PARP enables effective cross-lingual pruning (mask transfer) and multi-lingual subnetwork discovery, which are not addressed by prior pruning approaches.","PARP is applicable beyond speech SSL, showing effectiveness on pre-trained BERT/XLNet for natural language tasks."]}

methods: Prune-Adjust-Re-Prune (PARP): a magnitude-based unstructured pruning method for discovering and finetuning sparse subnetworks in pre-trained speech SSL models
Baseline pruning methods: One-Shot Magnitude Pruning (OMP), Iterative Magnitude Pruning (IMP) for comparison
Cross-lingual pruning (mask transfer): transferring subnetworks pruned for one language to another
Multi-lingual subnetwork discovery: finding a single subnetwork for multiple languages in one finetuning run
problems: Improving low-resource Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) using self-supervised speech representation learning (SSL)
Finding sparse subnetworks in pre-trained speech SSL models that outperform the original dense models for low-resource ASR
Reducing computational cost and inefficiency of existing pruning methods (e.g., OMP, IMP) for speech SSL models
Enabling efficient cross-lingual and multi-lingual ASR with sparse subnetworks

results.metric: Word Error Rate (WER) decrease (Librispeech 10min, no LM decoding)
results.value: 10.9%/12.6% absolute decrease compared to the full model using PARP

novelty_claims: PARP (Prune-Adjust-Re-Prune) is a new pruning method that discovers and finetunes sparse subnetworks in pre-trained speech SSL models with only a single downstream ASR finetuning run, unlike prior methods requiring multiple runs.
PARP allows pruned-out weights to be revived during finetuning, enabling minimal adjustment and better performance than OMP/IMP.
PARP achieves both computational efficiency and superior ASR performance (lower WER) compared to baseline pruning methods.
PARP enables effective cross-lingual pruning (mask transfer) and multi-lingual subnetwork discovery, which are not addressed by prior pruning approaches.
PARP is applicable beyond speech SSL, showing effectiveness on pre-trained BERT/XLNet for natural language tasks.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["DPHuBERT: joint distillation and pruning for self-supervised speech model compression"],"problems":["Large model size and computational cost of self-supervised speech models hinder deployment in resource-constrained environments","Existing knowledge distillation methods require manual design of student architectures, which can be suboptimal and require expertise","Previous pruning methods are task-specific and not task-agnostic, limiting their generalizability"],"datasets":["SUPERB benchmark"],"metrics":["Task performance metrics on SUPERB (e.g., accuracy, F1, WER, depending on the task)","Training time","Performance with limited training data"],"results":[{"metric":"SUPERB benchmark (various tasks)","value":"DPHuBERT outperforms pure distillation methods in almost all tasks"},{"metric":"Training time","value":"DPHuBERT requires little training time"},{"metric":"Performance with limited training data","value":"DPHuBERT performs well even with limited training data"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a task-agnostic compression method for speech SSL based on joint distillation and pruning (DPHuBERT)","Allows the student architecture to be learned during distillation, rather than being fixed and manually designed","DPHuBERT can be applied to various speech SSL models and performs well even with limited training resources","Outperforms prior distillation methods in almost all tasks on the SUPERB benchmark"]}

methods: DPHuBERT: joint distillation and pruning for self-supervised speech model compression
problems: Large model size and computational cost of self-supervised speech models hinder deployment in resource-constrained environments
Existing knowledge distillation methods require manual design of student architectures, which can be suboptimal and require expertise
Previous pruning methods are task-specific and not task-agnostic, limiting their generalizability
metrics: Task performance metrics on SUPERB (e.g., accuracy, F1, WER, depending on the task)

results.value: DPHuBERT outperforms pure distillation methods in almost all tasks
DPHuBERT performs well even with limited training data

novelty_claims: Proposes a task-agnostic compression method for speech SSL based on joint distillation and pruning (DPHuBERT)
Allows the student architecture to be learned during distillation, rather than being fixed and manually designed
DPHuBERT can be applied to various speech SSL models and performs well even with limited training resources
Outperforms prior distillation methods in almost all tasks on the SUPERB benchmark

methods: Knowledge distillation of Whisper model into a smaller Distil-Whisper model
Large-scale pseudo-labelling to assemble a diverse open-source dataset
WER-based heuristic for filtering high-quality pseudo-labels
Speculative decoding using Distil-Whisper as an assistant model to Whisper
problems: Deploying large pre-trained ASR models in low-latency or resource-constrained environments
Maintaining robustness and accuracy of ASR models after compression/distillation
Reducing hallucination errors in long-form audio transcription
Ensuring consistent transcription formatting across diverse datasets
datasets: Large-scale open-source pseudo-labelled dataset covering 10 distinct domains (assembled by the authors)
Four out-of-distribution test sets spanning multiple audio domains (not individually named in the excerpt)
metrics: Hallucination rate (implied by discussion of hallucination errors)

results.metric: Word Error Rate (WER) difference (OOD test sets)
Word Error Rate (WER) on long-form evaluation

results.value: 2x faster inference with only 8% increase in parameter count

novelty_claims: First to demonstrate robust knowledge distillation for large Seq2Seq ASR models like Whisper using large-scale pseudo-labelling.
Propose a simple yet effective WER-based heuristic for filtering pseudo-labelled data to ensure high-quality distillation.
Show that the distilled model maintains robustness to diverse and noisy acoustic conditions and is less prone to hallucination errors on long-form audio.
Introduce the use of Distil-Whisper as an assistant model for speculative decoding, achieving significant speed-ups while guaranteeing unchanged outputs.
Release of training and inference code, as well as models, to facilitate further research in speech model distillation.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Transformer encoder with Input-Dependent Dynamic Depth (I3D)","Local gate predictors for skipping self-attention or feed-forward blocks","Global gate predictor for dynamic depth selection at utterance or chunk level"],"problems":["High computational cost and large model size of Transformer-based end-to-end speech recognition models","Inefficiency of fixed-architecture compressed models for varying input difficulty","Suboptimal performance-computation trade-off in existing model compression and dynamic models for ASR"],"datasets":[],"metrics":[],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a Transformer encoder with input-dependent dynamic depth (I3D) for end-to-end ASR, allowing the model to adapt its depth based on input characteristics.","Introduces utterance-level (or chunk-level) gating to skip entire self-attention or feed-forward blocks, rather than frame-level or fine-grained operations.","Demonstrates that I3D models outperform both vanilla Transformers and static pruned models with similar inference-time layer counts.","Provides analysis of gate probabilities and input-dependency, offering insights into the behavior of deep encoders."]}

methods: Transformer encoder with Input-Dependent Dynamic Depth (I3D)
Local gate predictors for skipping self-attention or feed-forward blocks
Global gate predictor for dynamic depth selection at utterance or chunk level
problems: High computational cost and large model size of Transformer-based end-to-end speech recognition models
Inefficiency of fixed-architecture compressed models for varying input difficulty
Suboptimal performance-computation trade-off in existing model compression and dynamic models for ASR
novelty_claims: Proposes a Transformer encoder with input-dependent dynamic depth (I3D) for end-to-end ASR, allowing the model to adapt its depth based on input characteristics.
Introduces utterance-level (or chunk-level) gating to skip entire self-attention or feed-forward blocks, rather than frame-level or fine-grained operations.
Demonstrates that I3D models outperform both vanilla Transformers and static pruned models with similar inference-time layer counts.
Provides analysis of gate probabilities and input-dependency, offering insights into the behavior of deep encoders.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Systematic evaluation of 10 Speech Foundation Models (SFMs) for speech intelligibility prediction","A simple method that learns a lightweight specialized prediction head on top of frozen SFMs","Prediction head extracts multi-layer features from SFMs and uses cross-attention to exploit binaural cues","Ensembling SFMs by averaging predictions"],"problems":["Speech intelligibility prediction for hearing-impaired listeners","Non-intrusive prediction of percentage of words correctly perceived from speech-in-noise recordings","Lack of systematic benchmarks for SFMs on speech perception tasks"],"datasets":["Clarity Prediction Challenge 2 (CPC2) dataset"],"metrics":["Prediction accuracy (percentage of words correctly recognized)","Statistical significance of performance differences"],"results":[{"metric":"CPC2 Challenge outcome","value":"Winning submission"},{"metric":"Ensemble performance","value":"Over 60% of SFM ensembles outperform the best individual model; all ensembles outperform all but the single best model"}],"novelty_claims":["First systematic evaluation of 10 SFMs on a speech perception task (intelligibility prediction for hearing-impaired listeners)","Proposed a specialized prediction head leveraging multi-layer SFM features and cross-attention for binaural cues","Demonstrated that SFM ensembles learn complementary information and can outperform individual models","Winning method for the Clarity Prediction Challenge 2 (CPC2)"]}

A simple method that learns a lightweight specialized prediction head on top of frozen SFMs
Prediction head extracts multi-layer features from SFMs and uses cross-attention to exploit binaural cues
Ensembling SFMs by averaging predictions
problems: Speech intelligibility prediction for hearing-impaired listeners
Non-intrusive prediction of percentage of words correctly perceived from speech-in-noise recordings
Lack of systematic benchmarks for SFMs on speech perception tasks
metrics: Prediction accuracy (percentage of words correctly recognized)

results.value: Over 60% of SFM ensembles outperform the best individual model; all ensembles outperform all but the single best model

novelty_claims: First systematic evaluation of 10 SFMs on a speech perception task (intelligibility prediction for hearing-impaired listeners)
Proposed a specialized prediction head leveraging multi-layer SFM features and cross-attention for binaural cues
Demonstrated that SFM ensembles learn complementary information and can outperform individual models
Winning method for the Clarity Prediction Challenge 2 (CPC2)

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Structured pruning based on adaptive low-rank factorization (FLOP)","Augmented Lagrangian L0 norm regularization for pruning","Magnitude-based pruning strategy","Dynamic learning of embedding dimensions for word clusters"],"problems":["Reducing the size and inference latency of large language models","Improving the efficiency of model compression while maintaining performance","Overcoming hardware and software limitations of unstructured and block-structured pruning methods","Optimizing the trade-off between parameter reduction and model performance"],"datasets":["enwiki8","Wiki-103","GLUE benchmarks"],"metrics":["Perplexity","Compression ratio (parameter count reduction)","Inference speedup (training and inference time)","Downstream classification accuracy (on GLUE)"],"results":[{"metric":"Perplexity (enwiki8, 5M parameters)","value":"1.19"},{"metric":"Compression ratio (word-level language model with adaptive embeddings)","value":"50% compression with only 0.8 perplexity loss"},{"metric":"Inference speedup","value":"over 2x speed-up during both training and inference"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a generic, structured pruning approach based on adaptive low-rank factorization (FLOP) that does not require special hardware or software.","Introduces an improved L0 regularization technique using augmented Lagrangian methods to directly control compression level.","Demonstrates that low-rank factorization can outperform both block-structured and unstructured pruning in large language models.","Shows that dynamically learning embedding dimensions for word clusters leads to more optimal parameter-performance trade-offs.","Achieves significant compression and speedup with minimal performance loss, surpassing models of the same size trained from scratch."]}

methods: Structured pruning based on adaptive low-rank factorization (FLOP)
Augmented Lagrangian L0 norm regularization for pruning
Magnitude-based pruning strategy
Dynamic learning of embedding dimensions for word clusters
problems: Reducing the size and inference latency of large language models
Improving the efficiency of model compression while maintaining performance
Overcoming hardware and software limitations of unstructured and block-structured pruning methods
Optimizing the trade-off between parameter reduction and model performance

results.metric: Compression ratio (word-level language model with adaptive embeddings)

results.value: over 2x speed-up during both training and inference

novelty_claims: Proposes a generic, structured pruning approach based on adaptive low-rank factorization (FLOP) that does not require special hardware or software.
Introduces an improved L0 regularization technique using augmented Lagrangian methods to directly control compression level.
Demonstrates that low-rank factorization can outperform both block-structured and unstructured pruning in large language models.
Shows that dynamically learning embedding dimensions for word clusters leads to more optimal parameter-performance trade-offs.
Achieves significant compression and speedup with minimal performance loss, surpassing models of the same size trained from scratch.

methods: Self-supervised pretraining using BEST-RQ
Extension of joint training strategy JUST Hydra for finetuning with both source and unsupervised target domain data
Freezing the pretrained FM encoder during finetuning and only updating an added adapter for the target domain
Finetuning the FM encoder adapter and decoder to the target domain with a small amount of supervised in-domain data
Data selection from the source domain (YouTube) to mitigate domain gap
Utilizing audio-only or text-only data from the target domain for finetuning
problems: Efficient domain adaptation of speech foundation models to new target domains
Reducing the amount of supervised in-domain data and model parameters required for high-quality speech recognition
Improving data and model parameter efficiency in adapting large speech foundation models

metrics: Recognition quality (likely Word Error Rate, though not explicitly stated)
Amount of supervised in-domain data required

results.value: Same as baseline with much less data and parameters

novelty_claims: Proposes freezing the pretrained FM encoder during finetuning and only updating a lightweight adapter for the target domain, improving efficiency.
Demonstrates that proper data selection from the source domain can mitigate domain gap and improve target domain performance.
Shows that either audio-only or text-only data from the target domain can facilitate FM finetuning, even without paired data.
Introduces a unified and efficient framework for adapting speech foundation models to new domains with limited supervised data and model parameter updates.

Joint masked speech prediction and denoising in pre-training to improve both ASR and non-ASR task performance
Introduction of gated relative position bias in the Transformer for speech modeling
